parsed.internal_reasoning:
The artwork presented is visually identifiable as the *Salvator Mundi*, a depiction of Christ as the Savior of the World. Based on the visual evidence—the specific handling of the sfumato technique, the anatomical rendering of the right hand in benediction, the intricate ringlets of the hair, and the distinct blue robe with gold embroidery—this is the work attributed to Leonardo da Vinci, painted circa 1500. The prompt instructs me to assume authenticity; therefore, I am appraising this as a genuine Leonardo da Vinci, a category of object that represents the absolute apex of the art market.

Artistically, the piece demonstrates the master's signature techniques. The face exhibits the soft, smoky transitions of light and shadow (sfumato) that obscure the contours, giving the figure an ethereal, divine presence. The right hand is raised in a gesture of blessing, with the fingers positioned in a complex, anatomically precise manner that Leonardo studied extensively. The left hand holds a transparent rock crystal orb, symbolizing the heavens or the world. Notably, the orb lacks the optical distorting refraction one might expect from a solid crystal sphere, a feature that has sparked debate but is consistent with Leonardo's interest in optics and his desire to depict the orb as a symbol of perfection rather than a literal lens, or perhaps indicating it represents the celestial sphere which does not obey earthly physics. The drapery is rendered in deep ultramarine blue—a pigment more expensive than gold at the time—indicating a commission of significant importance.

Regarding condition, the image shows the painting in its post-restoration state (revealed publicly in 2011 and sold in 2017). The heavy overpainting that obscured the work for decades has been removed, revealing the original brushwork and the luminous quality of the face and hands. While there is some visible wear and abrasion, particularly in the dark background and the orb, the overall integrity of the composition is striking.

The market for Leonardo da Vinci is defined by extreme scarcity. There are fewer than 20 surviving paintings attributed to him, and almost all are held in major museums (Louvre, National Gallery London, Uffizi, etc.), making them effectively unbuyable. This leaves perhaps only one or two in private hands. The last time a Leonardo appeared at auction was this very painting, *Salvator Mundi*, which sold at Christie's New York in November 2017 for $450,312,500, setting the world record for the most expensive painting ever sold.

Valuing this piece today requires anchoring to that 2017 result while accounting for inflation and the unique nature of 'trophy assets.' While the broader art market has seen fluctuations, the ultra-high-end sector for 'Blue Chip' Old Masters remains resilient due to scarcity. A genuine Leonardo is not just a painting; it is a historical artifact of the highest order. Given the inflation since 2017 and the enduring fame of this specific image, the value would likely meet or exceed the previous record. I estimate the fair market value to be slightly above the 2017 hammer price, reflecting its status as the only available Leonardo on the market.
